{{Expand Japanese|賀茂那備神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamonahino Shrine | native_name = 賀茂那備神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Kamonahino Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kamonahino Shrine'''|賀茂那備神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Oki Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} * {{ill|Q135160342|lt=Q135160342|WD=Q135160342}} * {{ill|Q135026601|lt=Q135026601|WD=Q135026601}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11659043|lt=Q11659043|WD=Q11659043}} ** Series ordinal: 9 ** P155: {{ill|Uzukamikoto Shrine|lt=Uzukamikoto Shrine|ja|宇受賀命神社|WD=Q11449116}} ** P156: {{ill|Q24876393|lt=Q24876393|WD=Q24876393}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1063829|lt=Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}} * {{ill|Suki district|lt=Suki district|ja|周吉郡|zh|周吉郡|WD=Q10920245}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183000 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|WD=Q135159299}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|lt=Q17|WD=Q17}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町加茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:賀茂那備神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041142}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kamonabi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = Kamo 342, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}{{sfn|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Sacred object = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinai-sha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Established = |Main hall = {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = April 21{{ref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kamonabi
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kamonabi Shrine''' (かもなびじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kamo, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinai-sha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. === History === According to the “Kamo Daimyōjin Denki” (1742), during the “Tenpyō era” of {{jalink|Empress Kōken|Q232955}}{{efn|However, the era names during Empress Kōken’s reign were {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} and {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}}, while {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} was an era of the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|Shōmu}}.}}, Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto visited Kamo-ura in Oki Province and established the shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It is an ancient shrine listed in the “{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}” as “Kamonabi Shrine”{{efn|In the Yoshida family manuscript it appears as “Kamonamibi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. In the early modern period it was known as “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshū Shichō Gōki” (1667){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshūki” (1688), “Tōgo Kyūsha Torishirabechō” (1871), etc.{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, and in the “{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Inshū Jinmyōchō}}” it is listed as “Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Kamo Nabi Myōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}{{efn|This “Junior Fourth Rank Upper” is said to derive from a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. Among the Shikinai shrines of Oki Province, this shrine alone is a shrine of a transferred deity, leading to a theory that from the medieval period onward, a {{jalink|walking miko|Q11546650}} (the model for Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto mentioned below) promoted the idea that “Kamonabi is the Kamo deity,” and thus Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami came to be enshrined here{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|pp=289–290}}. : This deity was originally enshrined in Kyoto, but in seeking to cultivate new lands, shot an arrow adorned with leaves of the {{jalink|mallow family|Q156551|Aoi}} plant, deciding to travel to where the arrow landed{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=74}}. Following the arrow’s trajectory, the deity departed by boat from {{jalink|Wakasa Bay|Q11616636}} and arrived at the Oki Islands{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=74–75}}. The place where the arrow struck came to be called “Mato no Mae,” and a rock bearing the shape of a target can still be seen today{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}. The deity moored at “Funashima” and visited the Kamo settlement{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}, teaching the people weaving, fishing, shipbuilding, agriculture, and providing medical care{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=76–78}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Autogenerated pages with jawiki interwikis, possibly accidentally overwritten]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{Expand Japanese|賀茂那備神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamonahino Shrine | native_name = 賀茂那備神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Kamonahino Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kamonahino Shrine'''|賀茂那備神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Oki Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} * {{ill|Q135160342|lt=Q135160342|WD=Q135160342}} * {{ill|Q135026601|lt=Q135026601|WD=Q135026601}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11659043|lt=Q11659043|WD=Q11659043}} ** Series ordinal: 9 ** P155: {{ill|Uzukamikoto Shrine|lt=Uzukamikoto Shrine|ja|宇受賀命神社|WD=Q11449116}} ** P156: {{ill|Q24876393|lt=Q24876393|WD=Q24876393}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1063829|lt=Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}} * {{ill|Suki district|lt=Suki district|ja|周吉郡|zh|周吉郡|WD=Q10920245}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183000 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|WD=Q135159299}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|lt=Q17|WD=Q17}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町加茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:賀茂那備神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041142}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kamonabi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = Kamo 342, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}{{sfn|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Sacred object = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinai-sha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Established = |Main hall = {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = April 21{{ref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kamonabi
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kamonabi Shrine''' (かもなびじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kamo, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinai-sha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. === History === According to the “Kamo Daimyōjin Denki” (1742), during the “Tenpyō era” of {{jalink|Empress Kōken|Q232955}}{{efn|However, the era names during Empress Kōken’s reign were {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} and {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}}, while {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} was an era of the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|Shōmu}}.}}, Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto visited Kamo-ura in Oki Province and established the shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It is an ancient shrine listed in the “{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}” as “Kamonabi Shrine”{{efn|In the Yoshida family manuscript it appears as “Kamonamibi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. In the early modern period it was known as “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshū Shichō Gōki” (1667){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshūki” (1688), “Tōgo Kyūsha Torishirabechō” (1871), etc.{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, and in the “{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Inshū Jinmyōchō}}” it is listed as “Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Kamo Nabi Myōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}{{efn|This “Junior Fourth Rank Upper” is said to derive from a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. Among the Shikinai shrines of Oki Province, this shrine alone is a shrine of a transferred deity, leading to a theory that from the medieval period onward, a {{jalink|walking miko|Q11546650}} (the model for Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto mentioned below) promoted the idea that “Kamonabi is the Kamo deity,” and thus Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami came to be enshrined here{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|pp=289–290}}. : This deity was originally enshrined in Kyoto, but in seeking to cultivate new lands, shot an arrow adorned with leaves of the {{jalink|mallow family|Q156551|Aoi}} plant, deciding to travel to where the arrow landed{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=74}}. Following the arrow’s trajectory, the deity departed by boat from {{jalink|Wakasa Bay|Q11616636}} and arrived at the Oki Islands{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=74–75}}. The place where the arrow struck came to be called “Mato no Mae,” and a rock bearing the shape of a target can still be seen today{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}. The deity moored at “Funashima” and visited the Kamo settlement{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}, teaching the people weaving, fishing, shipbuilding, agriculture, and providing medical care{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=76–78}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

{{Expand Japanese|賀茂那備神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamonahino Shrine | native_name = 賀茂那備神社 | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Kamonahino Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kamonahino Shrine'''|賀茂那備神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Oki Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} * {{ill|Q135160342|lt=Q135160342|WD=Q135160342}} * {{ill|Q135026601|lt=Q135026601|WD=Q135026601}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11659043|lt=Q11659043|WD=Q11659043}} ** Series ordinal: 9 ** P155: {{ill|Uzukamikoto Shrine|lt=Uzukamikoto Shrine|ja|宇受賀命神社|WD=Q11449116}} ** P156: {{ill|Q24876393|lt=Q24876393|WD=Q24876393}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1063829|lt=Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}} * {{ill|Suki district|lt=Suki district|ja|周吉郡|zh|周吉郡|WD=Q10920245}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183000 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|WD=Q135159299}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|lt=Q17|WD=Q17}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町加茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:賀茂那備神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041142}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kamonabi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = Kamo 342, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}{{sfn|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Sacred object = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinai-sha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Established = |Main hall = {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = April 21{{ref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kamonabi
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kamonabi Shrine''' (かもなびじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kamo, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinai-sha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. === History === According to the “Kamo Daimyōjin Denki” (1742), during the “Tenpyō era” of {{jalink|Empress Kōken|Q232955}}{{efn|However, the era names during Empress Kōken’s reign were {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} and {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}}, while {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} was an era of the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|Shōmu}}.}}, Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto visited Kamo-ura in Oki Province and established the shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It is an ancient shrine listed in the “{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}” as “Kamonabi Shrine”{{efn|In the Yoshida family manuscript it appears as “Kamonamibi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. In the early modern period it was known as “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshū Shichō Gōki” (1667){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshūki” (1688), “Tōgo Kyūsha Torishirabechō” (1871), etc.{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, and in the “{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Inshū Jinmyōchō}}” it is listed as “Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Kamo Nabi Myōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}{{efn|This “Junior Fourth Rank Upper” is said to derive from a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. Among the Shikinai shrines of Oki Province, this shrine alone is a shrine of a transferred deity, leading to a theory that from the medieval period onward, a {{jalink|walking miko|Q11546650}} (the model for Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto mentioned below) promoted the idea that “Kamonabi is the Kamo deity,” and thus Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami came to be enshrined here{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|pp=289–290}}. : This deity was originally enshrined in Kyoto, but in seeking to cultivate new lands, shot an arrow adorned with leaves of the {{jalink|mallow family|Q156551|Aoi}} plant, deciding to travel to where the arrow landed{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=74}}. Following the arrow’s trajectory, the deity departed by boat from {{jalink|Wakasa Bay|Q11616636}} and arrived at the Oki Islands{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=74–75}}. The place where the arrow struck came to be called “Mato no Mae,” and a rock bearing the shape of a target can still be seen today{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}. The deity moored at “Funashima” and visited the Kamo settlement{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}, teaching the people weaving, fishing, shipbuilding, agriculture, and providing medical care{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=76–78}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kamonahino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

It is located in [[Oki Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|WD=Q134917286}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|WD=Q845945}} * {{ill|Shikinai Shosha|lt=Shikinai Shōsha|en|Shikinai Shosha|ja|式内小社|simple|Shikinai Shosha|WD=Q134917287}} * {{ill|Q135160342|lt=Q135160342|WD=Q135160342}} * {{ill|Q135026601|lt=Q135026601|WD=Q135026601}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11659043|lt=Q11659043|WD=Q11659043}} ** Series ordinal: 9 ** P155: {{ill|Uzukamikoto Shrine|lt=Uzukamikoto Shrine|ja|宇受賀命神社|WD=Q11449116}} ** P156: {{ill|Q24876393|lt=Q24876393|WD=Q24876393}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1063829|lt=Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}} * {{ill|Suki district|lt=Suki district|ja|周吉郡|zh|周吉郡|WD=Q10920245}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.188472|133.278334}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 183000 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|WD=Q135159299}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|lt=Q17|WD=Q17}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '島根県隠岐郡隠岐の島町加茂', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:賀茂那備神社]] {{wikidata link|Q135041142}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Kamonabi Shrine |Image = [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii |Location = Kamo 342, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}{{sfn|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Deity = Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Sacred object = |Rank = {{jalink|Shikinai-sha (minor shrine)|Q134917287}}, former {{jalink|Village shrine|Q134917284}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}} |Established = |Main hall = {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage status = |Annual festival = April 21{{ref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Kamonabi
Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kamonabi Shrine''' (かもなびじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kamo, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinai-sha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. === History === According to the “Kamo Daimyōjin Denki” (1742), during the “Tenpyō era” of {{jalink|Empress Kōken|Q232955}}{{efn|However, the era names during Empress Kōken’s reign were {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} and {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}}, while {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} was an era of the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|Shōmu}}.}}, Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto visited Kamo-ura in Oki Province and established the shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It is an ancient shrine listed in the “{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}” as “Kamonabi Shrine”{{efn|In the Yoshida family manuscript it appears as “Kamonamibi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. In the early modern period it was known as “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshū Shichō Gōki” (1667){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshūki” (1688), “Tōgo Kyūsha Torishirabechō” (1871), etc.{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, and in the “{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Inshū Jinmyōchō}}” it is listed as “Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Kamo Nabi Myōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}{{efn|This “Junior Fourth Rank Upper” is said to derive from a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. Among the Shikinai shrines of Oki Province, this shrine alone is a shrine of a transferred deity, leading to a theory that from the medieval period onward, a {{jalink|walking miko|Q11546650}} (the model for Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto mentioned below) promoted the idea that “Kamonabi is the Kamo deity,” and thus Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami came to be enshrined here{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|pp=289–290}}. : This deity was originally enshrined in Kyoto, but in seeking to cultivate new lands, shot an arrow adorned with leaves of the {{jalink|mallow family|Q156551|Aoi}} plant, deciding to travel to where the arrow landed{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=74}}. Following the arrow’s trajectory, the deity departed by boat from {{jalink|Wakasa Bay|Q11616636}} and arrived at the Oki Islands{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=74–75}}. The place where the arrow struck came to be called “Mato no Mae,” and a rock bearing the shape of a target can still be seen today{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}. The deity moored at “Funashima” and visited the Kamo settlement{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}, teaching the people weaving, fishing, shipbuilding, agriculture, and providing medical care{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=76–78}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kamonahino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kamonahino Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }} '''Kamonabi Shrine''' (かもなびじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in Kamo, {{jalink|Okinoshima Town|Q1205039}}, {{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}, {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinai-sha}} (minor shrine), and its former shrine rank was village shrine. === History === According to the “Kamo Daimyōjin Denki” (1742), during the “Tenpyō era” of {{jalink|Empress Kōken|Q232955}}{{efn|However, the era names during Empress Kōken’s reign were {{jalink|Tenpyō-shōhō|Q1373169}} and {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}}, while {{jalink|Tenpyō|Q1199879}} was an era of the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Shōmu|Q349263|Shōmu}}.}}, Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto visited Kamo-ura in Oki Province and established the shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It is an ancient shrine listed in the “{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}” as “Kamonabi Shrine”{{efn|In the Yoshida family manuscript it appears as “Kamonamibi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. In the early modern period it was known as “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshū Shichō Gōki” (1667){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, “Kamo Daimyōjin”{{efn|“Inshūki” (1688), “Tōgo Kyūsha Torishirabechō” (1871), etc.{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}.}}, and in the “{{jalink|Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|Inshū Jinmyōchō}}” it is listed as “Junior Fourth Rank Upper, Kamo Nabi Myōjin”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}{{efn|This “Junior Fourth Rank Upper” is said to derive from a {{jalink|Sōgen Senji}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. It has also been noted that compared to other shrines within Oki Province, this rank is low despite being a Shikinai shrine{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|p=290}}.}}. Since the Meiji period, it has reverted to the old name from the Engishiki and has been called “Kamonabi Shrine”{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. === Enshrined deities === === Principal deity === * '''{{jalink|Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami}}''' (わけいかづちのかみ) : The enshrined deity of {{jalink|Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine|Q700448}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|divided spirit|Q195793}} of Kamo Wakeikazuchi Shrine{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=965}}. Among the Shikinai shrines of Oki Province, this shrine alone is a shrine of a transferred deity, leading to a theory that from the medieval period onward, a {{jalink|walking miko|Q11546650}} (the model for Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto mentioned below) promoted the idea that “Kamonabi is the Kamo deity,” and thus Wakeikazuchi-no-Kami came to be enshrined here{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987b|pp=289–290}}. : This deity was originally enshrined in Kyoto, but in seeking to cultivate new lands, shot an arrow adorned with leaves of the {{jalink|mallow family|Q156551|Aoi}} plant, deciding to travel to where the arrow landed{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=74}}. Following the arrow’s trajectory, the deity departed by boat from {{jalink|Wakasa Bay|Q11616636}} and arrived at the Oki Islands{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=74–75}}. The place where the arrow struck came to be called “Mato no Mae,” and a rock bearing the shape of a target can still be seen today{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}. The deity moored at “Funashima” and visited the Kamo settlement{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=75}}, teaching the people weaving, fishing, shipbuilding, agriculture, and providing medical care{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=76–78}}. The people wished to repay the deity, and Kaede-hime, the most beautiful woman in the village, hoped to become the deity’s consort{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=78–79}}. The deity accepted her, and together they devoted themselves to helping the villagers{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|pp=79–80}}. As a renewed expression of gratitude, the villagers built a large palace{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. This is said to be the origin of the shrine; afterward, although the deity passed away, the soul is believed to still be enshrined here{{sfn|Yokoyama|1966|p=81}}. Such legends are preserved. === Associated deities === * '''{{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}''' (すさのおのみこと) * '''Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto''' (たまやすひめのみこと) : As mentioned above, she is a deity with characteristics similar to a walking miko who is said to have come to the island during Empress Kōken’s reign; her place of origin is unknown{{sfn|Rikahinosha|1987a|p=185}}. === Structures === [[File:Kamonabi Shrine Honden.jpg|thumb|Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine]] ; Main hall : Three bays wide (3.96 meters), two bays deep (3.17 meters), with a ridge height of approximately 8 meters from the ground{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Built in the {{jalink|Oki-zukuri|Q11659055}} style with a copper roof{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}, it features a karahafu gable at the center of the front porch{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. Constructed in Bunsei 11 (1828), it has been designated a town cultural property as described below{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}. ; Connecting hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|gabled style|Q13308575}}, with a floor area of 4 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Worship hall : Copper-roofed {{jalink|irimoya style|Q855025}}, with a floor area of 16 tsubo. Built in 1918 (Taishō 7){{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Treasure storehouse{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} ; Lodging for worshippers{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}} : In Oki, after praying at one’s tutelary shrine for recovery from illness, safe childbirth, a good catch, school admission, and the like, there was a custom called “Okomori,” in which one would spend the night in a hut within the shrine grounds called a “Komosha” or “Komoriya” as thanks for the fulfillment of the prayer{{sfn|Ōtsu|1973|p=310}}. ; Torii : A wooden Myōjin-style torii{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Stone lanterns : Four lanterns as of 1983{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Festivals === ; Grand festival : According to the early Meiji-era “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the first or middle Rooster Day of April{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Since the adoption of the solar calendar, the annual festival has been called the “Aoi Festival” and is held on April 21{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Minor festivals : According to the “Tōgo Shrine Investigation Register,” held on the 1st, 11th, and 21st of each month{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; New Year festival : January 7{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Hyakute Festival : March 11{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. Three boys whose parents are both alive shoot bows{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gion Festival : June 15{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozaide Festival : September 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity departing for Izumo during {{jalink|the Month Without Gods|Q6364021|the time when the gods gather in Izumo}}{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. ; Gozairi Festival : October 30{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. A festival marking the shrine’s deity returning from Izumo{{sfn|Handa|1983|p=966}}. === Cultural property designation === === Okinoshima Town–designated cultural properties (tangible cultural property) === * '''Main hall of Kamonabi Shrine''' – Designated on November 26, 2020{{sfn|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}} === Footnotes === === Notes === {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist|20em}} === Further reading === * {{cite book|Japanese|author=Takehisa Ōtsu|chapter=Shinto-Buddhist Rituals|editor=Takatoshi Ishizuka|title=Folklore of the Oki Islands: Emergency Survey Report on Oki Island Folk Materials|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|year=1973|pages=285–327|id={{NCID|BN13843154}}|ref={{sfnref|Ōtsu|1973}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yaichirō Handa|chapter=9 Kamonabi Shrine|editor=Shikinai Shrine Research Society|title=Survey Report on Shikinai Shrines, Vol. 21, San’in-dō 4|publisher=Kōgakkan University Press|year=1983|pages=963–966|id={{JP number|83032918}}|ref={{sfnref|Handa|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Yashirō Yokoyama|title=Legends of Oki|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9545363|publisher=Shimane Publishing and Culture Association|year=1966|ref={{sfnref|Yokoyama|1966}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=452. Tamayasu-hime-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/99|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987a}}}} Rikahinosha 1987a. * {{Cite book|Japanese|author=Rikahinosha|chapter=677. Wakeikazuchi-no-Mikoto|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/13275772/1/151|title=Gods of Shimane|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|id={{JP number|88012198}}|year=1987|pages=289–290|ref={{sfnref|Rikahinosha|1987b}}}} Rikahinosha 1987b. * {{cite web|title=Kamonabi Shrine – Shrine Details|url=https://www.shimane-jinjacho.or.jp/okinoshima/03ce65b64ca85ba5b2920065092122571cbeab5a.html|publisher=Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Shimane Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines}}}} * {{cite web|title=Town-designated Cultural Properties (Tangible Cultural Properties) │ Okinoshima Town – A Moment of Rest, an Island of Peace|url=https://www.town.okinoshima.shimane.jp/www/contents/1001000000254/index.html|publisher=Okinoshima Town Board of Education|accessdate=2025-10-01|ref={{sfnref|Okinoshima Town Board of Education}}}} === See also === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai shrines in Oki Province|Q11659043}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kamonabi Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kamonahino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kamonahino Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Oki Province]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:島根県の旧村社]] [[Category:隠岐国の式内社]] [[Category:隠岐の島町の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]
